segments.text:
Human Demography
Learn how to use demography data to calculate
Survival rate (lx)
Death rate ( qx )
Life expectancy (ex)
Learn how to construct survivorship/death graphs.  When to use Log scale.
Compare survivorship/death data across populations to learn about links between demography, population dynamics, and life history characteristics.

Age class
(in 3-month intervals)
(x) | Number alive at start of age class x
(nX) | Proportion alive at start of age class x
(lX) | Deaths from age class
x to x+1
(dX) | Death rate
(qX) | Average life expectancy at start of age class x 
(eX)

Table 3.1  Partial cohort life table for meadow grass ( Poa annua ) in England.
Note this column is arbitrary
These sort of numbers are not very intuitive, so we change them to lx or Nx
843/843=1.0

More standardized data form that can be used to compare with other populations

of age class

to be lived

Table 3.4.  Intermediate columns used in calculating life expectancy, e X , for meadow grass ( Poa annua ) from Table 2.1.  An example of interpreting life expectancy data would be that meadow grass that survived to age class 2 (6 months) would be expected to live on average 1.51 more age classes (or 4.5 more months).

Note – no title for figures
What is missing from this graph?

Use log scale on Y-axis
Number of Survivors (log)
Using log scale on the y-axis allows you to show percent change
Same data on survivorship graphed on linear and logarithmic scale
Number survivors
Number survivors (log)
Age (years)

Tips in answering questions
Example: effect of pH on Hydra survival
First describe the general pattern, then use a specific example to back up
Include data AND explain data
“ Hydra showed the highest survival at pH 4 (0.90±0.15), which is 2-fold of that at pH 7 (0.45±0.09) and 3-fold of that at pH 10 (0.30±0.03)”.

Weak answers and strong answers
Question: 	Find an area of the life table where males and females differ in mortality.  Describe the difference and support with data.
Weak answer :  Females had lower mortality rates than males.
Better answer: Male mortality rates were generally higher than female mortality rates, with the notable exception of the 20-29 year age class, when female mortality (0.116) was approximately two times higher than male mortality (0.056).
